topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored envelope sat on the worn oak desk, a stark intrusion into the otherwise muted grey of the room. Elias traced the embossed seal with a fingertip, the paper feeling brittle beneath his touch. It had been fifteen years since the argument, fifteen years since the sharp words had hung in the air like smoke, leaving behind a residue of profound stillness. He hadn't expected anything. The handwriting on the page was elegant, formal, a stark contrast to the messy, panicked scrawl of his own regret. He unfolded the letter, the silence in the apartment amplifying the rustle of the heavy paper. Reading the carefully constructed prose, a tight knot formed beneath his ribs. He didn't move to tear it up; instead, he placed it carefully inside a drawer, the metallic click echoing loudly in the quiet. A slow, heavy sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed to carry the weight of all those intervening years.

[story 2]

Clara stared out the rain-streaked window, the city lights blurring into streaks of neon. The letter arrived by post, an unexpected anomaly in her carefully managed routine. It was a formal apology, dated from the very moment of the transgression. She picked it up, her fingers trembling slightly as they brushed the thick cardstock. For a long moment, she didn't read, just holding the paper as if it might dissolve. When she finally did, a sudden, sharp sensation prickled behind her eyes. She dropped the letter onto the sofa cushion, the sound barely audible. Her shoulders slumped, the tension she hadn't realized she was holding dissolving into a heavy, languid droop. She rose slowly, walking to the kitchen, pouring herself a glass of water, the cool liquid offering no solace.

[story 3]

Marcus found the letter tucked beneath a stack of old tax documents. He picked it up, his gaze skimming over the familiar script, searching for a flaw, a hint of insincerity. The paper felt strangely warm in his hand. The memory of the incident surfaced, unwelcome and unwelcome, bringing a tightening sensation in his chest. He reread the lines several times, his jaw clenching so hard his teeth ached. When he finally set the letter down, he didn't look at it again. He turned abruptly, pacing the length of his living room three times before stopping by the window. The view outside seemed distant, muted, as if the world itself had dimmed in response to the contents of that single piece of correspondence.
